coredata.dc:title: MXenes as theranostics: Diagnosis and therapy including in vitro and in vivo applications
coredata.dc:creator: Aslam, Maira
Ahmad, Tahleel
Manzoor, Muhammad Husnain
Laiba,
Verpoort, Francis
coredata.dc:description: In the present era, there is a remarkable fascination with two-dimensional (2D) structures owing to their immense potential in diverse scientific applications. These applications include biomedical devices, nanomedicine, biosensors, biomembranes, and energy storage. These structures are attractive because of their physicochemical features. Thinness gives them a high surface-to-volume ratio, enabling fast biomolecule attachment and adjustable surface changes. MXenes have great photothermal energy conversion potential. They are effective CT and MRI contrast materials due to their diversified metal elements. With their large specific surface area, impressive mechanical properties, favorable electrical conductivity, and photothermal behavior, MXenes have many biomedical applications, particularly in cancer therapy. MXenes have been shown to be useful in theranostics, biosensors, antimicrobial treatments, pharmaceuticals, bioimaging, regenerative medicine, and tissue engineering. These materials have shown promise in vivo and in vitro cancer models. The present article discusses MXenes top-down and bottom-up synthesis as well as their features including electrical, mechanical, optical, magnetic, and thermal reliability. It also discusses theranostics, biosensors, therapeutics, CT imaging, photoacoustic imaging, bioimaging, tissue engineering, fluorescent imaging, cancer theranostics, the role of MXenes in different therapies, nano-oncology contributors, and other biomedical applications. Lastly, a comprehensive discussion is provided on the MXene-based biomaterials’ present challenges and potential prospects particularly in the realm of theranostics.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present era, there is a remarkable fascination with two-dimensional (2D) structures owing to their immense potential in diverse scientific applications. These applications include biomedical devices, nanomedicine, biosensors, biomembranes, and energy storage. These structures are attractive because of their physicochemical features. Thinness gives them a high surface-to-volume ratio, enabling fast biomolecule attachment and adjustable surface changes.

MXenes have great photothermal energy conversion potential. They are effective CT and MRI contrast materials due to their diversified metal elements. With their large specific surface area, impressive mechanical properties, favorable electrical conductivity, and photothermal behavior, MXenes have many biomedical applications, particularly in cancer therapy.

MXenes have been shown to be useful in theranostics, biosensors, antimicrobial treatments, pharmaceuticals, bioimaging, regenerative medicine, and tissue engineering. These materials have shown promise in vivo and in vitro cancer models. The present article discusses MXenes top-down and bottom-up synthesis as well as their features including electrical, mechanical, optical, magnetic, and thermal reliability. It also discusses theranostics, biosensors, therapeutics, CT imaging, photoacoustic imaging, bioimaging, tissue engineering, fluorescent imaging, cancer theranostics, the role of MXenes in different therapies, nano-oncology contributors, and other biomedical applications.
Lastly, a comprehensive discussion is provided on the MXene-based biomaterials’ present challenges and potential prospects particularly in the realm of theranostics.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no new data were created or analyzed in this study.

ce:section.ce:para: Across the globe, there is a growing recognition of the imperative for sustainable development [1–3]. Two-dimensional materials have garnered considerable attention in the scientific field's due to their remarkable catalytic, electronic, magnetic, mechanical, and photonic properties, which made their distinction from their bulk counterparts [4,5]. Various 2D materials, graphene has emerged as a strong contender for numerous applications [6–8]. Since 2004, several atomically thin 2D materials have been successfully isolated or synthesized [9–11], including transition metal oxides/hydroxides, transition metal dichalcogenides, iodinene (2D iodine), bismuthine, Boron nitride nanosheet [12], phosphorene, tellurene, antimonene (2D antimony), metal sulfides, MXenes, selinene, and others [13,14].

In the realm of nanomedicine and biomaterials, the emergence of two-dimensional (2D) materials proposes a promising avenue to overcome the limitations associated with conventional theranostic materials [15]. Conventional theranostic materials are used for both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. However, they often suffer from a number of drawbacks, including low biocompatibility [16]. Low drug loading capacity, poor targeting ability along with low sensitivity and specificity. 2D materials have the capability to address many of the limitations of conventional theranostic materials. They are biocompatible, highly sensitive and specific, and have high drug loading capacity [17].

Among them, MXenes have grabbed notable attention as a 2D inorganic chemical family comprised of atomically thin layers of transition metal carbides/nitrides and carbonitrides [18–20]. MXenes exhibit unusual physicochemical properties that make them particularly appealing for biological applications [21]. These properties include their large surface area, hydrophobicity, metallic conductivity, tunable surface chemistry, and optical properties [22]. In addition to these general properties, MXenes also have a number of other properties that are relevant for biological applications, such as biocompatibility, antibacterial properties and antioxidant properties [23,24].

Their compatibility with various hydrophilic terminations and the ease of synthesizing MXenes were first discovered by Professors Y. Gogotsi and M. W. Barsoum and their colleagues in 2011 [25,26]. MXenes are derived by selectively etching the corresponding 3D MAX phases, removing the main-group sp-elements (denoted as A in MAX), typically III or IV A, while preserving the non-corrodible d-block transition metals like Ti (denoted as M in MAX), along with C or N (denoted as X in MAX) [27–29]. MXenes can be classified into different types, including 2–1 MXenes, 3–2 MXenes, 4–3 MXenes, and 5–4 MXenes, with double transition metal MXenes such as 2–1–2 MXenes and 2–2–3 MXenes also being reported [30]. While computational predictions have proposed over 75 potential MXene compositions, more than 45 MXenes and over 100 MAX phases have been successfully synthesized [31–33].

Analyzing the complicated surface of MXenes reveals that their structural details disclose fascinating depth. MXenes are layered solids comprise of several bonded layers. When the number of layers is less than five, the as-prepared HF-etched MXenes exhibit an accordion-like structure and are commonly referred to as multi-layer MXenes (ML-MXene) [34]. MXene surfaces can be terminated by numerous functional groups. MXenes are derived from their parent MAX phases, having general formula Mn+1XnTx. Here, "M" represents transition metals, "X" represents carbon and nitrogen elements, "T" represents surface termination groups such as -F, -O, and -OH, and "n" can be 1, 2, or 3. The letter "A" denotes Group III/IVA elements [35–37]. The first MXene, Ti3C2Tx, was obtained by selectively etching an aluminum atomic layer from Ti3AlC2 [38].

Due to their fascinating and distinct electrical, optical, mechanical, and biological properties, MXenes hold great potential as alternative biomaterials for a broad range of biomedical processes [39,40]. Key utilization of MXenes in biomedicine include tissue engineering, antibacterial characteristics, biosensing, cancer treatment, and drug delivery [41,42].

MXenes possess ample surface functional groups such as -F, -OH, and -O, which contribute to their hydrophilicity, a crucial feature in biomedicine [43,44]. Most MXenes have zeta potential values that are more negative than -79.9 mV, typically between -30 mV and -80 mV [45]. One example of a MXene with a zeta potential value of around -79.9 mV is Ti3C2Tx in water [46,47]. MXenes display improved biodegradability, distinguishing them from many other 2D materials when exposed to oxygen (O2) and water (H2O). Due to this characteristic, MXenes are especially attractive for applications where environmental interactions are crucial. The enhanced biodegradability of MXenes makes them stand out as 2D material alternatives, but it also suggests that they might be environmentally beneficial and sustainable in a range of technological applications [48,49]. The distinctive combination of metallic conductivity resulting from the free electrons in the transition metal carbide and nitride backbone, along with the prevalence of C, N, H, and O—common components in living beings—further adds to the biocompatibility of MXenes [50–52]. MXenes' remarkable photothermal energy conversion capability enables their utilization in second near-infrared (NIR) bio-window, which facilitates deep tissue penetration and finds applications in photonic hyperthermia treatment [53,54]. Additionally, MXenes' diverse range of metal elements makes them suitable as contrast agents for computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging [50,55]. However, it is important to note that the synthesis of MXenes alone may not fully meet the desired qualities and performance requirements of nanomedicine and biomaterials, as our understanding of pathological features continues to evolve [56]. Exploiting MXenes' light-harvesting capabilities and broad-spectrum absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region, which encompasses both the first and second NIR biological windows, holds significant potential for photoacoustic imaging and photothermal treatment [57–59]. In the domain of cancer related nano-theranostics, MXenes as well as their composites have gained attention due to their thermal, electronic, optical, and intriguing mechanical properties, offering opportunities for targeted cancer nano-therapy and precise identification of cancer cell locations [60]. Advanced MXene-based structures have shown promise in various applications, including photothermal therapy, chemodynamic therapy, photodynamic therapy, targeted anticancer drug delivery, computed tomography imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging [61]. These systems exhibit lower toxicity, multifunctionality, biocompatibility, enhanced targeting, and improved solubility [62].

The development of MXene-based biomaterials has followed a trajectory from exfoliation to the utilization of modest organic molecules, followed by ions with small radii such as Li+, and eventually large molecules with long carbon chains as intercalators [63]. MXenes have shown promise as materials for capacitors, batteries, and other energy-related applications [64–66].

This review extensively furnishes an overview of the advancement in MXenes-based biomedical applications [67]. It commences with an introduction and brief background of MXenes, proceeded by description of their structure, synthesis methods, novel properties mainly drug holding capacity, tribology, antibacterial activity, and biomedical applications with a particular emphasis on their role in theranostics for diagnosis, therapies and nanooncology treatment [42,68]. Furthermore, diagnostic applications are thoroughly explored including a thorough comparison of 0D, 1D, 2D nanomaterial, biosensors, CT imaging, photoacoustic imaging, fluorescent imaging, MRI imaging, and computed tomography imaging [69]. Afterwards, recent use of MXenes in various therapies; chemotherapy, phototherapy, photothermal therapy, and radiotherapy and how the antibacterial activity is achieved by using these therapies are explained [70]. Subsequently, discussing MXenes substantial role in nano-oncology treatment, bone tissue regeneration, sensing cancer cells, synergistic therapies and particularly in breast cancer [56]. Moreover, the review focuses on the ways of improving MXenes stability, MXenes benchmarking with other advanced 2D materials notably MBenes, and the progress of MXenes and other 2D materials in HER [71–73]. Finally, reflecting the current state of research, challenges encountered by MXenes as theranostics agents and their future potential are highlighted [74].

Significant advancements in synthetic MXene methods have made the synthesis of novel MXenes feasible [75]. Generally, there are two types of techniques employed: top-down approaches and bottom-up approaches. The top-down approach involves the separation of bulk materials, such as MAX phases, into several layers, while the bottom-up method uses the MXene deposition technology, forming the material from the bottom to the top [76]. Top-down synthesis approaches are the most commonly used and recognized method for synthesizing MXenes because of their scalability and relatively low cost, see Fig. 1
[77]. Bottom-up synthesis approaches are more complex, time-consuming and result in lower yield of MXene sheets. In addition, the bottom-up approach is more controllable than the top-down approach [78]. Top-down approach also uses hazardous chemicals, such as hydrofluoric acid (HF). Bottom-up approach produces high-quality MXene sheets with fewer defects but it is not widely used due to the complicated procedures [79].

Selective etching using a top-down approach is widely employed in MXene synthesis. This fabrication technique has demonstrated scalability, without any loss or alteration of properties when the batch size is increased [80]. Strong etching solutions containing fluoride ions, such as ammonium bifluoride (NH4HF2), hydrofluoric acid (HF), and a mixture of lithium fluoride (LiF) and hydrochloric acid (HCl), are typically used to etch MAX phases and produce MXenes, see Fig. 2
[24]. For instance, when Ti3AlC2 is etched in aqueous HF at room temperature, the A (Al) atoms are preferentially removed, resulting in the annihilation of carbide layers' surfaces by OH, O, and F atoms [41]. Lewis acid molten salts, e.g., ZnCl2, can be employed to achieve Cl termination and generate MXenes. The Cl-terminated MXene maintains its structural integrity up to 750 ℃ [81]. Various traditional Lewis acid molten salts, including CdCl2, FeCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, AgCl, and NiCl2, have been effectively used to etch most members of the MAX phase, including those with A elements Ga, Si, and Zn [38].

The synthesis of MXene Ti4N3 differs from that of carbide MXenes as it is the first reported nitride MXene. In this case, a mixture of molten eutectic fluoride compounds, such as potassium fluoride, sodium fluoride, and lithium fluoride, is combined with MAX phase Ti4AlN3 and heated to generate Ti4N3. The etching of Al from Ti4N3 produces multilayered Ti4N3. By immersing the MXene in tetrabutylammonium hydroxide followed by sonication, single-layer and multilayer Ti4N3 can be obtained [38,52].

Di Wang et al. synthesized DS-Ti2CCl2 MXene through a reaction involving Ti, TiCl3, and graphite powders under Ar atmosphere. The powders, in a molar ratio of 4:2.2:2.7 for Ti, TiCl3, and graphite, respectively, were finely crushed. The resulting powder was then compacted into a pellet using a hydraulic press at approximately 0.77 GPa of pressure. This pellet was vacuum-sealed in a quartz ampoule. The reaction was initiated by placing the ampoules in a muffle furnace, and the required reaction temperature was achieved in 20 minutes. The temperature was maintained at 950 ℃ for 2 hours, ensuring the completion of the reaction and the maximum yield of MXene. Subsequently, the ampoules were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature [83].

In the work by Ding et al. [84], Ti3C2Cl2 MXene was prepared through the conversion of MAX. Ti3AlC2 and CdCl2, in a molar ratio of 1:2, were ground in a glove box under Ar protection using an agate mortar. The resulting mixture was transferred outside the glove box and placed in an alumina crucible. This crucible was then positioned in a tube furnace and heated for 5 hours at 700 °C. After reaching room temperature, the alumina crucible was washed with deionized water to remove any residual salts. Additionally, HCl or (NH4)2S2O8 solution was employed to eliminate the metal produced during the reaction. The mixture underwent filtration and multiple washes with deionized water to remove any remaining chemical reagents, resulting in the final production of MXene.

MXenes have been extensively researched and show promise in various fields [85,86]. They have been utilized as high photothermal conversion efficiency transparent conductors [87–89], field-effect transistors [90,91], supercapacitors [92–94], catalysts [86,95], shields for electromagnetic interfaces [87,96,97], polymer composites with fillers [98,99], filtration systems, multifunctional coatings [89], substances for storing methane [100,101], Lithium-ion batteries [92], surfaces capable of proper dye adsorption [102], composite nanomaterials [103,104], photocatalysts for hydrogen production [105,106], ceramic biomaterials for cancer therapy [67,107], electronic devices [93,94,108,109], magnetic applications [110,108], optical devices [89], thermoelectric systems [109,111], sensing devices [86,112], hydrogen storage medium [113,114], catalysis, photocatalysis [115], and related processes [92,86,95], superconductivity at the nanoscale [116,117]. Materials that can be used as topological insulators characterized by wide band gaps involving only d orbitals, materials with very low work function [89], junctions formed by Schottky diodes [94].

High-surface-area MXenes and similar nanocomposites possess excellent physicochemical characteristics [118–120]. They have a large capacity for loading of drugs [120,118] and offer therapeutic possibilities for various groups [39]. MXenes have the advantage of simplicity in synthesis and the ability to be functionalized [119,121]. They exhibit pH-responsive drug behavior [39,44,122]. MXenes have been studied for their potential in photomedicine, where they act as materials with greater light-to-heat conversion efficiency and photosensitization capabilities, enabling easier utilization of photons in medical imaging and treatment of various diseases [123]. Non-invasive theranostic approaches combining photothermal and photodynamic therapies have been developed for the detection and management of different malignancies [124]. MXenes also have applications in magnetic treatments with remotely controlled drug/gene delivery [125].
MXenes exhibit several remarkable features, such as their role in tribology, cancer treatment, and antibacterial activity, which are described below with illustrative examples.

In tribology applications, MXenes have gained substantial importance due to their unique properties, including chemical reactivity, layered 2D structure, tunability, and high-temperature and high-pressure tolerance compared to other 2D materials. In environments devoid of oxygen and water, they can withstand temperatures up to 900 °C, with their carbides transforming into 3D crystalline bulk carbides. This transformation into useful oxides can be achieved through interactions with specific substrates, counterbodies, and environmental conditions. Notably, MXenes exhibit features like strong substrate adherence, long-term low friction, and low wear, resulting in the formation of in situ tribofilms [126].

Furthermore, MXenes have demonstrated the ability to function as superlubricous and zero-friction 2D materials. For instance, a multilayer Ti3C2Tx−MoS2 mixture was spray-coated onto abrasive 52,100 grade steel surfaces to serve as a solid lubricant. Tribological assessments were conducted in a ball-on-disk configuration using unidirectional sliding. The experimental results confirmed that Ti3C2Tx−MoS2 composites could attain superlubricous states [127].

In the realm of energy generation, triboelectric nanogenerators hold great potential for powering smart internet devices due to their low-frequency operational range, ease of integration, and cost-effectiveness. To maximize their performance and durability, it is crucial to enhance their properties. Researchers have explored the triboelectric properties of doped and undoped hydrogenated DLC films, multi-layer Ti3C2Tx films, and MoS2 coatings in contact separation and freestanding sliding modes. It was found that MXenes, with their functional oxygen and fluorine groups, outperformed other films in contact separation [128].

MXenes also play a significant role in cancer treatment and tumor ablation. Notably, MXenes have demonstrated their effectiveness in photothermal therapy (PTT). Experiments involving MXene-facilitated PTT on two distinct breast cancer cell models, namely conventional spheroids and bio-printed spheroids, revealed a significant reduction in cell survival rates and increased ROS generation following radiation. This underscores the robust ability of MXenes in non-invasive PTT on 3D conventional spheroids, with bio-printed spheroids exhibiting even greater efficacy due to the presence of the extracellular matrix, which enhances thermal conduction and faithfully replicates the effectiveness of PTT in vivo [129].

Moreover, a study has explored the photothermal antibacterial properties of few-layer (FX) and multi-layer (MX) Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. MXenes, owing to their reversible bacteria-trapping capacity, have demonstrated superior efficacy in blocking the growth of bacteria such as S. aureus and E. coli. Treatment with 25 µg/ml of MX resulted in the survival of only 37% of E. coli and 23% of S. aureus cells, in contrast to FX, where the effect was less pronounced, allowing 72% of E. coli and 46% of S. aureus cells to survive. When the concentration was increased to 100 μg/ml, the survival rates dropped to just 11% of E. coli and 4% of S. aureus. Furthermore, simultaneous administration of 100 μg/ml MX and NIR laser (5.7 Wcm−2) for 5 minutes completely eliminated both species. In comparison, for FX with concentration of 100 μg/ml with NIR (3 Wcm−2), the antibacterial effect was less pronounced, resulting in the destruction of 87% of E. coli and 95% of S. aureus, see Fig. 3 . These findings highlight the superior antibacterial activity of MX over FX [130].

MXenes' biocompatibility and adjustable electrical and optical characteristics have led to their exploration in cancer theranostics [131]. In this field, MXenes have been integrated into nanocomposites and microsystems with unique mechanical, electrical, optical, and targeting properties. These materials hold high potential for both diagnosis and therapy [118]. In addition to photothermal and photodynamic therapies, MXenes can be used for cancer imaging [124]. They enable targeted drug release and imaging of tumors and other cancers. MXene-based systems demonstrate favorable biological compatibility [41] and possess properties that allow cellular uptake and targeted delivery, such as preferential uptake by capillary endothelial cells. Their autofluorescence properties enable tracking and monitoring after administration. Surface engineering of MXenes can further enhance their biological suitability [118], focusing on sustainability, biocompatibility [132,133], besides optical, mechanical, and electrochemical properties, thereby facilitating the development of multifunctional cancer theranostic systems [42]. An overview of MXenes applications in the biomedical field is given in Fig. 4 .

Researchers have been exploring various nanomaterials and nanoarchitectures with acceptable efficiency and biocompatibility for biomedical and clinical applications. MXenes' unique physiochemical properties not only make them attractive as anticancer drug deliverynanocarriers and nanoplatforms, as well as for diagnostic and biosensing applications [134]. The versatile nature of MXenes enables their adaptation for different oncological applications, including photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and cancer imaging and theranostics (such as CT/MRI/PA imaging) [135].

MXenes large surface area offers effective loading of various functional molecules, such as chemotherapeutic drugs, fluorescent probes, and biomacromolecules, through covalent or noncovalent interactions [136,137]. MXenes can also adsorb functional nanoparticles (NPs), including Au NPs, Fe3O4 NPs, and inorganic quantum dots, on their surface, imparting additional properties such as magnetic and radioactive functions for diverse diagnostic applications. Furthermore, MXenes exhibit substantial near-infrared light absorption, making them suitable for photothermal treatment (PTT) of cancer. Due to these unique characteristics, 2D nanomaterials, including MXenes became known as promising nanoplatforms for a variety of cancer theranostic applications. Recent progresses in the biomedical use of 2D materials, particularly for theranostics, have been systematically compiled, highlighting their potential in this field [138].

Biosensors are analytical tools that detect biological processes by generating distinct signals. They consist of a physical-chemical converter and a biological receptor [139,140]. The potential of biosensors to replace expensive and bulky classical analytical equipment in healthcare has been widely explored [141]. Currently, there is a focus on developing biosensors for the precise and rapid monitoring of chemicals or biomolecules associated with chronic diseases and adverse effects, which can improve early diagnosis and treatment [142]. The COVID-19 pandemic has pointed out the potential of biosensors in combating such pandemics and expediting treatment [143].

The success of biosensors heavily relies on the characteristics of the materials used in their construction [144]. The history of biosensing materials is extensive, encompassing various 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials, such as MoS2, nanorods, and transition metal nanoparticles [145].

Zero-dimensional nanomaterials are often referred to as nanoparticles. They have a size range typically less than 100 nanometers in all dimensions. Due to their small size, 0D nanomaterials exhibit quantum confinement effects, leading to unique electronic and optical properties. One-dimensional (1D) nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal nanowires, and macromolecule nanofibers, have overcome challenges faced by conventional biosensors made of bulk materials. Many 1D nanomaterials are excellent conductors of electricity. Their increased specific surface area and rapid charge transfer mechanisms have contributed to exceptional biosensing performance [146]. However, the commercial potential of 1D materials has been limited due to their expensive manufacturing processes, unpredictable behavior in different device modules, and instability under various conditions [147].

2D nanomaterials are atomically thin and have a planar structure. They can be incredibly strong and flexible, with properties like high tensile strength. Some 2D materials, such as graphene, exhibit high electron mobility, making them promising for electronics. Their remarkably high surface-to-volume ratio can make them highly reactive, useful for catalysis and sensing.

In contrast, MXenes offer streamlined production capabilities, the ability to retain physicochemical, electrical, and surface properties, and rich surface functionalities, making them highly attractive for biosensor construction. There have been multiple discoveries regarding the use of 2D materials, including MXenes, in creating biomarkers or detectors that can qualitatively and quantitatively detect metabolic abnormalities, assess the potency of therapies, and identify environmental hazards such as infections and toxic compounds [139,140,146,148]. MXenes and their derivatives have recently gained recognition in the field of biosensing due to their extraordinary sensitivity, detection range, limit of detection (LOD), and stability. Xu et al. [149] demonstrated neuronal activity detection using Ti3C2−MXene micropatterns, creating field-effect transistor (FET) biosensors, see Fig. 5
[145].

Indeed, MXene-based sensors can be created by physically attaching target molecules to the surface of MXene materials. Physical adsorption relies on the attractive forces between the target molecules and the MXene surface, enabling their attachment without the need for covalent bonds or specific functionalization. This approach offers simplicity and flexibility in sensor fabrication.

In MXene-based biosensors, the advantages of Ti3C2−MXene multilayer systems have been highlighted. These systems have been employed through physical adsorption to immobilize electrochemically active proteins for sensing various analytes such as nitrites, insecticides, H2O2, and phenol [150]. For instance, Liu Hui et al. used MXene to immobilize hemoglobin (Hb) in the creation of biosensors without the need for intermediaries [150]. The 2D multilayer MXene-Ti3C2 nanomaterials can provide a protective microenvironment to keep the proteins active and stable. Additionally, the high surface area and unique structure of MXene-Ti3C2 facilitate the concentration of the substrate, making it more accessible to the immobilized enzyme within the nanomaterial [151,152].

The developed biosensor using MXene-Ti3C2 has demonstrated excellent reproducibility, a vast linear range, low limit of detection (LOD), and stability. It has been successfully validated for the rapid identification of phenol in tap water, yielding satisfactory results. This mediator-free biosensor based on MXene-Ti3C2 offers a simple, accurate, fast, and cost-effective method for phenol detection in water. The use of MXene materials for enzyme immobilization shows great potential for various applications in environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnostics.

However, while physical adsorption is an effective technique for sensing, it does not fully exploit the immense potential of MXenes' two-dimensional nanosheet structure combined with their high surface group density. The detection efficiency of physical adsorption methods is limited when detecting trace amounts of biomarkers [151].

Salama and colleagues have described the functionalization of MXene nanosheets with amino silane, see Fig. 6 , which can be utilized for the attachment of antibodies and electrochemical detection of cancer biomarkers. To adapt the characteristics of MXenes for particular purposes, chemical functionalization of MXenes entails altering the surface of nanosheets made of MXene with different chemical groups or molecules. MXenes are two-dimensional transition metal carbides, nitrides, or carbonitrides that commonly have hydroxyl, oxygen, or fluorine groups as their surface terminations. MXenes' adaptability and performance in a variety of applications can be improved via chemical functionalization. The primary focus of chemical functionalization is to modify the surface of MXene nanosheets. [153]. The surface of single- or multilayer MXene (Ti3C2) was chemically modified to introduce amino groups, allowing for the covalent immobilization of biological receptors. This covalent immobilization of DNA, enzymes, and proteins enhances the efficiency of biomolecule loading, facilitates quicker analyte access, improves regularity and delivery, and enables higher density of specific biomarkers, thereby significantly enhancing the performance of biosensors.

In the specific case of cancer biomarker detection, the biofunctionalized MXene device showed a broad linear detection range for Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), which is a key biomarker associated with lung, pancreatic, colorectal, ovarian, and breast cancer. The use of MXene-based biosensors holds promise for the sensitive and accurate spotting of cancer biomarkers, aiding in early diagnosis and effective treatment [151].

MXenes, a class of 2D materials, have shown great potential in computed tomography (CT) imaging due to their superior X-ray absorption properties. Compared to other materials, MXenes exhibit enhanced X-ray attenuation, leading to improved contrast in CT images. Enhanced X-ray attenuation refers to an increase in the ability of a material to absorb or scatter X-rays compared to the surrounding tissue or medium. To attain improved contrast imaging, it typically involves the injection of a high concentration or volume of contrast agents into the patient, followed by immediate imaging [154]. This enhanced contrast facilitates the detection of tumors and other abnormalities within the body, making CT imaging a valuable medical imaging technique [155].

Even while computed tomography (CT) may not be directly related to MXenes, their potential chemical and structural variety may nonetheless have an impact on CT and other imaging modalities in a number of ways. MXenes have the potency to be engaged in CT imaging as contrast agents. The contrast in CT scans can be improved by MXenes because of their excellent X-ray attenuation capabilities. In vivo surface modification of MXene (Ti3C2) nanosheets with tumor photothermal treatment together with simultaneous MR/CT imaging abilities was described by Zong et al. using GdW10 nanoclusters (as contrasting agents). Further research should be done on these biocompatible composites for multipurpose nanotheranostics, particularly for targeted cancer treatment and detection [156].

CT contrast agents (CAs) are often substances with higher atomic numbers (Z) that are used to further enhance the contrast in CT images. For example, WS2-polyethylene glycol, which contains the element tungsten-74 with a higher atomic number than iodine-53 commonly used in CT imaging, has been successfully established as a CT contrast agent [157].

Another promising candidate for CT contrast agents is bismuth-83, which exhibits favorable biocompatibility and has a higher atomic number. In particular, 2D topological Bi2Se3 nanosheets have demonstrated improved CT imaging of in vivo tumor tissue due to their substantial X-ray attenuation properties (atomic number 73) [158,125].

Tantalum (Ta)-based compound Ta4C3 MXenes have also been identified as potential CT contrast agents. The surface-modified Ta4C3 (Ta4C3-SP) has shown significant signal enhancement and improved contrast in CT imaging compared to commercial iodine-based contrast agents. In in vitro experiments using Xanthan gum, higher levels of Ta4C3 -SP led to an important signal enhancement in the Hounsfield unit value and associated CT images, see Fig. 7
[159]. Moreover, in vivo testing on tumor-bearing mice, intravenous administration of Ta4C3-SP resulted in a significant contrast enhancement of the tumor in CT scans taken 24 hours after injection, with a noticeable increase in Hounsfield units. 83 ± 8.8 to 232.3 ± 25.2 HU of significant tumor contrast enhancement may be seen in CT scans taken 24 hours after injection. These findings indicate that Ta4C3 MXenes hold promise as potential CT contrast agents for future imaging applications [159].

Photoacoustic (PA) imaging is an emerging biomedical imaging technique that combines the advantages of both optical and ultrasound imaging. It offers high spatial and temporal resolution, making it a favorable modality for various applications [160,161]. The principle behind PA imaging is based on the photoacoustic effect, initially described by Alexander Graham Bell [162].

In PA imaging, short laser pulses are used to illuminate tissue. When the laser light is absorbed by the tissue, it generates a rapid thermoelastic expansion, resulting in the emission of ultrasonic waves. These ultrasonic waves are then detected by an ultrasonic transducer, which converts them into electrical signals. These signals are processed to reconstruct a PA image [163].

The effectiveness of PA imaging lies in the absorption coefficient of light in biological tissue. It allows for imaging with high contrast and sensitivity to tissue structures, similar to optical imaging. However, PA imaging overcomes the limited penetration depth of pure optical imaging by incorporating the ability of ultrasound to penetrate deeper into tissue [163]. By combining the strong contrast of optical imaging and the deep penetration of ultrasound, PA imaging provides a unique imaging modality [164].

Compared to other optical imaging technologies, PA imaging has the advantage of imaging deeper tissues. This makes it particularly useful for applications such as tumor imaging, functional brain imaging, and vascular imaging, where imaging deep within the body is essential, see Fig. 8 . PA imaging holds great promise in advancing biomedical imaging capabilities and improving our understanding and diagnosis of various diseases [164].

In order to ensure and increase the image quality in photoacoustic imaging (PAI), MXenes can be used as contrast enhancers. One approach is to coat MXenes with a layer of gold, which enhances their luminescence and increases the generation of sound waves when they are illuminated. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have long been used as contrast agents in PA imaging due to their ability to exhibit localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) phenomenon, resulting in strong optical absorption and tunability [165].

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), and other imaging modalities may all benefit from the use of contrast agents, especially when combined with metallic nanoparticles. Because of their superparamagnetic characteristics, iron oxide nanoparticles (IONP) have attracted a lot of attention as contrast agents. Clinical applications for superparamagnetic IONPs (SPIONs) include local hyperthermia, magnetically targetable medication delivery, and usage as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [166].

AuNPs not only enhance contrast in PA imaging but also exhibit significant photothermal conversion effects upon laser irradiation. They possess high biocompatibility, chemical inertness, favorable physiological characteristics, and a high extinction coefficient, making them versatile for imaging applications [167].

The cylindrical shape of gold nanorods (AuNRs) plays a role in their absorption band. As the aspect ratio (length to width) of the nanorods increases, their absorbance tends to shift towards the near-infrared (NIR) region. Therefore, AuNRs are widely utilized in the development of PA contrast agents due to their straightforward synthesis and adaptable absorption behavior in the NIR range [168].

By leveraging the unique properties of MXenes and gold-based contrast agents, researchers can enhance the photoacoustic signals and improve the sensitivity and specificity of PA imaging, enabling better visualization and characterization of biological tissues and structures.

The biomedical industry has increasingly adopted photoacoustic (PA) imaging for various applications. PA imaging offers the ability to study the physiological characteristics, morphological structure, pathological features, and metabolic functions of biological tissues. It provides efficient scanning of cellular composition and function, making it particularly suitable for early cancer detection and treatment monitoring. PA imaging is widely used in bioimaging applications such as imaging lymphatic nodes, blood vessels, malignancies, and brain activity.

It is crucial to compare MXenes with other state-of-the-art 2D nanomaterials, such as graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), and black phosphorus (BP), in order to gain a comprehensive understanding of MXenes' potential as versatile 2D nanomaterials in various applications. Each of these materials possesses distinct characteristics and attributes that render them suitable for diverse purposes. Among them, graphene and MXene have drawn significant attention due to their exceptional architectures and properties.

Graphene and MXene share common features such as a high aspect ratio, active chemical surface, and a wide range of production methods. These shared attributes make them particularly well-suited for the creation of high-efficiency microwave absorption (MA) structures, including lightweight assemblies and diverse hybrids. However, it is worth noting that their differing conductivities, both functionally and structurally, lead to the development and utilization of MA materials in distinct manners [169].

In addition to bioimaging, photoacoustic techniques are utilized in biosensing applications. PA-based biosensors enable the detection of various analytes, including metal ions, pH levels, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) generation, and oxygen levels in the blood. The ability of PA imaging to provide both structural and functional information makes it a valuable tool in biomedical research and clinical applications, offering non-invasive and real-time monitoring capabilities, see Fig. 9
[164].

The versatility of PA imaging and its ability to provide detailed information about biological processes make it a promising technology in the field of biomedicine, with potential applications in disease diagnosis, treatment guidance, and monitoring of therapeutic responses. Ongoing research and development in PA imaging techniques and contrast agents, such as MXenes and gold-based nanoparticles, continue to expand its capabilities and enhance its clinical utility.

MXenes have emerged as versatile materials for fluorescent imaging due to their tunable optical properties. By introducing different functional groups onto MXene surfaces, the emission wavelength can be adjusted to match the absorption spectrum of specific target molecules. This capability enables the detection of target molecules in biological and environmental samples.

In comparison to conventional organic fluorophores, inorganic two-dimensional nanomaterials and quantum dots, including MXenes, offer several advantages for biological imaging. They exhibit attractive fluorescence properties such as adjustable frequencies, excellent photostability, and desirable quantum yields. For instance, graphene quantum dots (QDs), the first metal-free luminescent nanomaterial, have demonstrated promising applications in fluorescent imaging [170]. Similarly, graphene-like carbon nitride quantum dots (g- C3N4 QDs) and Ti3C2 MXene-based quantum dots (Ti3C2 MQDs) with high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields have been developed for intracellular bioimaging [171,172].

Hydrophilic and monolayer Ti3C2 MQDs have been synthesized using the hydrothermal method. These MQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior and display a significant quantum yield [173]. Cellular uptake of these MXene-based quantum dots occurs via endocytosis pathways [173], further highlighting their potential for applications in optical sciences, healthcare, and cell imaging.

Furthermore, Geng and colleagues have developed methods to synthesize ultrasmall MXene molecules, such as Ti3C2 MXenes, using intralayer slicing and breakdown techniques under mild conditions [174]. These ultrasmall MXenes exhibit light emission with significant excitation-dependent absorbance. Recent studies have also explored the application of solvothermal-treated Ti3C2 Tx MXene in dimethylformamide (DMF) for cellular imaging [175].

The utilization of MXene-based quantum dots as reagents for multicolor cell imaging and their potential across various disciplines underscore their significant role in fluorescent imaging and their prospects for further advancement in optical sciences and biomedical applications.

Molecular imaging technology plays a crucial role in tumor identification and prognosis monitoring, offering high reliability and accuracy [176,177]. To complement photoacoustic tomography, various multimodal imaging techniques are being developed, including single photon emission computed tomography, optical imaging, positron emission tomography, X-ray computed tomography (CT), ultrasound imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [178].

Manganese (Mn)-based magnetized substances, on e of the significant critical trace substances in the body of a person, have been considered as replacements for the conventional Gd-based contrast agents. For example, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has authorized the therapeutic use of MnCl2 tetrahydrate, an oral contrasting agent. Therefore, it is anticipated that the logical combination of Mn-based medicines with titanium dioxide, MXenes would enable simultaneous magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and photothermal tumor eradication. For effective tumor theranostics, the intricately crafted MnOx/Ti3C2 hybrid nanosheets are endowed with a number of unique properties. The pH-sensitive contrast agents used for T1 weighted magnetic resonance (MR) imaging may first be found on the outer layer of Ti3C2 nanosheets where MnOx constituents were in situ generated.

MRI has emerged as a highly effective non-invasive method for clinical detection and prognosis assessment, thanks to its excellent three-dimensional resolution, lack of ionizing radiation, and ability to differentiate between soft and hard tissues [179]. However, magnetic resonance imaging has limitations in effectively distinguishing normal and malignant tissues due to modest tissue variations resulting from the prolonged relaxation time of water protons and suboptimal tissue representation [180]. Contrast-enhancing magnetic resonance contrast agents (CAs) have been developed to enhance the contrast and improve the diagnostic effectiveness of MRI, as CAs are required in approximately 35% of clinical magnetic resonance studies [181]. Extensive research has been conducted to explore MRI CAs for enhanced molecular imaging [182].

T1-MRI or T2-MRI CAs have been developed based on manganese (Mn), gadolinium (Gd), and iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) to enhance imaging contrast sensitivity [183]. Gd-based T1 CAs in the form of ionic complexes have found wide applications in clinical settings [184]. However, conventional small-scale complex-based agents often exhibit in vivo toxicity and short blood circulation times, potentially leading to brain deposition and nephrogenic systemic fibrosis. Consequently, superparamagnetic nanoparticles, particularly Fe3O4 NPs, have garnered significant attention. Some T2-MRI CAs based on Fe3O4 NPs have already undergone clinical trials or received approval from the National Institutes of Health over the past two decades [185].

Nevertheless, the clinical applications of these nanoparticles have been limited due to inherent dark signals and susceptibility to MRI artifacts, making it challenging to differentiate small early-stage tumors from hypointense regions. In this regard, Mn-based CAs have emerged as promising alternatives due to their strong biocompatibility and excellent signal characteristics [186].

In recent years, the field of nanotechnology has witnessed remarkable growth, leading to the emergence of various multidisciplinary scientific disciplines, including bionanotechnology, nanotechnology in medicine for cancer, nanotechnology in healthcare, and nanobiotechnology [187]. Numerous nanomaterials and nanoarchitectures have been developed with the aim of achieving biocompatibility and effectiveness for diverse biomedical and clinical applications. These materials have captured the attention of scholars and scientists as innovative tools for gene delivery, nanocarriers, nanoplatforms, and anticancer drugs, particularly in the field of cancer diagnosis and therapy [188]. However, significant challenges remain in terms of their durability, reliability, degradability, performance, drug release, and specificity for clinical use in cancer treatment and assessment [189].

Current cancer treatments include radiation, surgery, immunotherapy, hormone-driven therapy, and combination therapy. However, chemotherapeutic and anticancer medications may not selectively or sensitively target the intended cells or tissues [190]. MXenes and MXene-based nanostructures find applications in diverse areas such as energy preservation, biomedicine, electrochemistry, pharmaceuticals, drug delivery, cancer therapy, theranostic nanotechnology, tissue engineering and regeneration, and water purification. These materials possess unique characteristics including high surface area, electrical conductivity, and facile construction of nanoscale structures [191]. MXenes, owing to the presence of several functional groups like oxygen, fluorine, and hydroxyl groups, exhibit inherent water solubility and potential for surface modification. The large surface area of MXenes allows for the incorporation of large substances for synergistic therapy, including immunostimulants, photosensitizers, and chemotherapeutic drugs [192].

Extensive research is being conducted on these nanomaterials for their potential applications in drug delivery, photothermal effects, and multimodal tumor treatment. Surface charge engineering plays a vital role in controlling the physical and chemical properties as well as surface characteristics of MXenes and their derived materials, which is crucial for their biological, biotechnological, and biomedical applications [193]. Particularly in the areas of tissue engineering, cancer nanotherapy and regenerative medicine, MXene-based nanodevices and nanoplatforms should possess superior targeting capabilities, efficacy, high reliability, enhanced biocompatibility under physiological conditions, and controlled release of drugs or other therapeutic or diagnostic agents [194]. For instance, composite hydrogels based on cellulose and Ti3C2 MXene have been developed with fast responses to near-infrared stimulation, serving as multifunctional nanoplatforms for intratumoral tumor ablation with doxorubicin hydrochloride. These hydrogels have demonstrated significant acceleration in drug release, see Fig. 10 [194].

The unique properties of these 2D structures hold great promise for effective and non-invasive cancer therapy and detection. They have been recognized for photothermal treatment (PTT) and controlled delivery of anticancer drugs. MXenes are frequently employed in biomedical imaging and biosensors due to their optical characteristics. These materials exhibit exceptional photothermal conversion efficiency, making them highly biocompatible and effective for PTT applications. This perspective highlights current advancements in the use of MXenes for specific cancer treatment and diagnostics, along with the associated challenges and promising prospects. To develop effective anticancer formulations/systems, important factors to consider include particle size, shape, stability (both external and internal), drug transport, targeting, and biocompatibility. Furthermore, comprehensive in vivo and in vitro studies are essential to objectively assess the biological compatibility, safety, and toxicity of MXenes and their derived materials for biomedical applications, particularly in cancer chemo-PTT. Toxicological and safety assessments are necessary to identify potential health risks and adverse reactions [195].

The compatibility of nanostructures with biological systems should be thoroughly examined for biomedical or clinical applications, as they may induce unfavorable biological or pharmacological effects due to their unique physicochemical properties [196]. Additionally, the chemical agents used in combination with MXenes and their derivatives may be hazardous and toxic, necessitating systematic and analytical evaluations to mitigate or eliminate these toxicity concerns [197].
The increasing popularity of graphene-based materials, including MXenes, can be attributed to their remarkable physicochemical properties and their potential as theranostic agents.

MXenes exhibit a unique combination of properties that make them highly attractive for various applications. They possess high electrical conductivity, excellent mechanical strength, large surface area, and chemical tunability, which allow for versatile functionality and customization [198]. These characteristics make MXenes suitable for diverse biomedical applications, particularly in the field of theranostics.

Theranostics refers to the integration of therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities into a single platform. MXenes have gained significant attention in this area due to their ability to combine therapeutic functionalities with diagnostic imaging capabilities. They can be functionalized with specific molecules, such as anticancer drugs, targeting ligands, or imaging agents, to achieve targeted drug delivery and imaging [199]. MXenes can be engineered to exhibit controlled drug release properties, enabling precise delivery of therapeutics to the desired site. Moreover, their inherent optical properties make them suitable for various imaging modalities, including fluorescence imaging, photoacoustic imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging [200].

The use of MXenes as theranostic agents holds great promise in cancer treatment and diagnosis. They can be utilized for precise tumor targeting, enhanced drug delivery, and real-time monitoring of therapeutic responses. By integrating therapeutic and imaging functionalities, MXenes enable personalized medicine approaches, where treatment strategies can be tailored based on individual patient characteristics and disease progression [199]. Overall, the exceptional physicochemical properties of graphene-based materials, such as MXenes, make them highly versatile and valuable for theranostic applications, see Fig. 11 . Ongoing research and development in this field are expected to further advance the use of MXenes as innovative theranostic platforms for improved cancer management and patient care.

Surface-modified MXenes have emerged as a promising class of materials with remarkable physicochemical properties and theranostic capabilities. Extensive research has been conducted to investigate their potential applications in cancer therapy. Notably, studies have explored the surface-modified effects of Ti3C2@FACisplatin and Ti3C2-Fe3O4@ SiO2-FACisplatin on cervical cancer tissue, both in vitro and in vivo, see Fig. 12 [201]. These investigations have demonstrated that both formulations effectively reduce cancer cell viability and enhance cytotoxicity in cervical tissues.

Furthermore, in vivo experiments have shown that compared to conventional chemotherapy using cisplatin alone or Ti3C2@Cisplatin, Ti3C2-Fe3O4@SiO2-FA-Cisplatin significantly increases the percentage of necrosis in cervical tissue, indicating its potential as an effective treatment option [201].

Surface-modified MXenes have also been explored as nanoplatforms for efficient drug delivery, leveraging their capacity for drug loading, large drug-releasing surfaces, and excellent biocompatibility. An example of such a system is the MXene@Au-PEG-DOX system, where MXenes are surface-modified with thiol polyethylene glycol aldehyde chains (SH-PEG-CHO) attached to Au particles. This synergistic system combines photothermal therapy (PTT) and chemotherapy for tumor eradication, exhibiting good photothermal stability and compatibility [202].

Another approach involves the development of cancer cell membrane-camouflaged nanocomplexes that specifically deliver cisplatin (CDDP) and programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1)-targeted antagonistic DNA aptamers (Apt) to tumor sites. These nanocomplexes efficiently release therapeutic agents and ferrous ions, elevating intracellular H2O2 levels and initiating the Fenton reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+. This reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and enhances chemotherapeutic efficiency, contributing to effective cancer treatment [203].

Ti3C2 MXenes have shown promise in on-demand drug delivery and release, pH sensitive, and near-infrared laser-triggered drug release, making them suitable for combined chemotherapy and photothermal ablation [204]. With extraordinary high drug loading capacities of up to 200% and together with strong photothermal properties, Ti3C2 MXenes possess the potential for effective tumor eradication, see Fig. 13 .

Moreover, studies have explored the effectiveness of mesopore-coated 2D Nb2C MXenes in treating the U87 cancer cell line and related tumor xenografts, demonstrating a high inhibitory ability in the NIR-II biowindow [205]. Surface engineering of 2D MXenes has also been investigated to expand their biological applications and improve cancer therapy outcomes.

In the field of antibacterial applications, Ti3C2 MXenes have been coupled with ciprofloxacin (Cip) to efficiently capture and eliminate bacteria. By incorporating Cip-Ti3C2 nanocomposites into a hybrid hydrogel, both chemotherapy and photothermal therapy are combined, resulting in remarkable in vitro bactericidal effectiveness (>99.999%) against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). In a murine abscess model caused by MRSA, the hybrid hydrogel exhibits long-term inhibitory effects, preventing bacterial rebound and maximizing the therapeutic efficacy of Ti3C2 [206].

Furthermore, surface-modified CaO2-loaded 2D titanium carbide nanosheets (CaO2-TiOx@Ti3C2) display intriguing sonodynamic and chemodynamic properties, see Fig. 14 . These nanosheets exhibit acoustic sensitization properties through the in-situ oxidation of Ti3C2 induced by CaO2, generating an acoustic sensitizer TiO2. They also support a Fenton reaction by self-supplied H2O2, further enhancing their chemodynamic properties. Consequently, the C-T@Ti3C2 nanosheets promote the generation of reactive oxygen species through sonodynamic therapy, prompting potent antibacterial effects and enhancing bone tissue regeneration [207].

Moreover, nanosheets based on molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), generated through ionic liquid exfoliation and surface modification, have demonstrated potential in cancer therapy. IL-MoS2-PEG-b-PIL nanosheets, with an adjacent dimension of approximately 96 nm and 4 nm in width, exhibit stable photothermal performance and photothermal effects. The addition of PEG-b-PIL polymers enhances the biocompatibility and biodegradability of the nanosheets. In vitro and in vivo studies have shown that combining chemotherapy and photothermal therapy using IL-MoS2-PEG-b-PIL nanosheets, along with 808 nm laser irradiation, synergistically inhibits tumor cell growth. Importantly, the nanosheets demonstrate good safety profiles, as evidenced by the steady weight gain observed in mice during treatment, further supporting their potential for clinical cancer therapy [208].

These studies underscore the significant potential of surface-modified MXenes and other nanosheet-based systems for efficient drug delivery, enhanced cancer therapy, and improved treatment outcomes. Continued research in this field aims to further optimize these nanoplatforms and explore their clinical applications.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a minimally invasive therapy that utilizes nanoagents capable of converting near-infrared (NIR) light radiation into hyperthermia, leading to ablation of tumor tissues. The performance of photothermal agents in PTT is influenced by two key factors: their ability to absorb light, quantified by the extinction coefficient (ε), and their capacity to generate heat in response to external light, known as the photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE, η). Various types of photothermal materials, including carbon-based materials, plasmonic metallic nanoparticles, inorganic multifunctional materials, and organic materials, have been investigated for their potential to improve photothermal conversion performance. However, PTT's wide applicability has been limited by low PCE and relatively low extinction coefficients. Recently, 2D MXenes have emerged as promising candidates for efficient photothermal agents due to their significant NIR absorption capabilities [125].

For instance, a biomimetic photoinduced plasmonic assembly (NPD@M) has been established, incorporating Nb2C plasmon MXene, Pt nanoenzyme, doxorubicin (DOX), and tumor cytomembrane. Under NIR-II laser irradiation, MXenes with photothermal therapy (PTT) enhance the efficiency of Pt nanoenzyme, resulting in a decrease in cell survival to 38.67% and enhanced tumor inhibition in vivo [209]. Vanadium MXenes (V2N) have been explored as a nano platform for PTT to increase the dual nature of enzymes in anti-infective therapy [210]. A Ti3C2 MXenes, collagen, silk fibroin, and quercetin composite scaffold (M-CSQ scaffold) have been developed for PTT in combination with a scaffold, which effectively destroys OSCC cells and promotes the repair of mucosal wounds [211]. M5C4(Mo4VC4) prepared under a synergistic scheme of PTT and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) exhibits bactericidal properties [212]. Metal-polyphenol nanodots impregnated on Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets have been shown to modify PTT and inhibit inflammation [213]. 2D Nb2C MXenes demonstrate a photothermal conversion performance of 28.6% under near-infrared-II bio-window irradiation at 1064 nm, thus improving photothermal hyperthermia and contributing to tumor elimination [214]. Transparent MXenes have been utilized in smart contact lenses for ocular photothermal therapy, ensuring safety [214]. Mo2Ti2C3 (MTC) MXene nanoparticles have exhibited photothermal properties and effectively eliminated methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) under photothermal therapy. Both in vitro and in vivo studies showed that the antibacterial activity of MTC+NIR groups to be significantly higher compared to control groups (PBS+NIR, MTC, and MTC+NIR). In in vivo antibacterial activity assay compared to the PBS and MTC groups, the MTC+NIR group's wound size significantly decreased after day 10 at rates of 5, 45, and 60 cm2, see Fig. 15 . After 10 days, the mice were euthanized, and the afflicted tissues were removed for the bacterial count. This was done to further quantify the antibacterial performance. The MTC group had 89.46% fewer live bacteria than the PBS group, and the MTC+NIR group had 5.62% fewer, which clearly depicts the outstanding photothermal efficiency of the MTC+NIR group against the MRSA, see Fig. 16 [215].

Ti2C3-SP nanosheets possess a high extinction coefficient (ε) of 25.21 g−1cm−1 and effective absorption in the range of 400 to 900 nm, indicating their strong capability for near-infrared (NIR) laser absorption [216]. Upon irradiation with a laser for 6 minutes, the temperature of the nanosheets reached 55 ℃, highlighting their excellent photothermal properties as a result of the generated heat. In vitro photothermal (PT) performance was assessed by subjecting a group of 4T1 cells to hyperthermia, which resulted in the ablation of the cells, see Fig. 17 . Similarly, the nanosheets demonstrated tumor ablation in xenograft tumors in vivo, providing strong evidence of their remarkable photothermal efficiency [125].
Moreover, a flexible breathable epidermal sensor capable of ultrasensitive sensing and immediate photothermal therapy has been designed using MXene nanosheets and silver nanowires (AgNWs) [217].

Radiotherapy is a technique that involves delivering high-energy X-ray irradiation doses to tumor sites to destroy cancer cells by causing damage to their DNA or generating free radicals [218]. While radiotherapy is an essential part of cancer treatment, its limited specificity can result in harm to surrounding healthy tissues. To address this challenge, 2D materials such as MXenes have been extensively explored as radio enhancers due to their remarkable biocompatibility with mammalian cells. Recent research has demonstrated that 2D titanium carbide flakes, specifically Ti3C2Tx, offer much better radio-enhancement properties in comparison to oxidized MXenes and commercial titanium dioxide nanoparticles [219].

A photothermally responsive theranostic system has been developed by integrating phase change material (PCM)-gated Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with resveratrol (RSV). This system enables synergistic radiosensitization to optimize radiotherapy and prevent metastasis of orthotopic breast cancer [220].

The radiosensitivity of breast cancer cells (BCC) was studied under X-ray irradiation using two carbon-based nanoparticles, graphene oxide (GO), and Ti3C2 MXene. Both nanoparticles demonstrated BCC proliferation inhibition without causing significant cell toxicity. However, Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets exhibited a higher rate of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation (75.2% for MXene and 65.2% for GO), leading to superior radio sensitization efficiency compared to GO. Overall, the results suggested that MXene nanoparticles could be an optimal choice for enhancing radiotherapy over GO nanoparticles [221], see Fig. 18 .

Ionizing radiation is widely used in radiotherapy and various industries, but repeated exposure to it can cause tissue damage. Niobium carbide, a 2D MXene material, acts as a radioprotector by effectively scavenging free radicals generated by ionizing radiation. The fascinating antioxidant properties of 2D Nb2C MXenes enable the efficient removal of H2O2, . OH, and O2
. − . Functionalizing Nb2C nanosheets with polyvinyl pyrrolidine (PVP) (Nb2C-PVP NSs) significantly reduces the production of reactive oxygen species induced by ionizing radiation, leading to increased cell survival in vitro. Moreover, a single intravenous injection of Nb2C-PVP in mice subjected to radiation enhances their survival rates to 100% and 81.25% when exposed to 5 Gy and 6.5 Gy of radiation, respectively. Additionally, Nb2C-PVP administration helps the hematopoietic system of irradiated mice recover, and it effectively reduces radiation-induced damage by enhancing superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity and reducing malondialdehyde levels in various organs. Nb2C-PVP is mostly cleared from the mouse body within 14 days after treatment, and no abnormalities are observed during the remaining 30-day period, see Fig. 19 [222].

2D vanadium carbide (V2C) can mimic the activity of six naturally occurring enzymes: superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), peroxidase (POD), glutathione peroxidase, thiol peroxidase, and halo peroxidase. V2C MXenzyme demonstrates adequate in vitro cytoprotective effects against oxidative stress, mitigates ROS-induced damage, and exhibits favorable therapeutic effects with remarkable biocompatibility in vivo [223].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a type of phototherapy that aims to induce cell death by generating reactive oxygen species. It has been established as a clinically approved approach for the treatment of various malignant disorders, including cancer, although it is still considered a relatively new therapeutic modality [224].

Ti3C2 MXene, a novel multifaceted material, possesses several cutting-edge qualities and shows potential for multimodal tumor therapy. Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets which are ultrathin have been prepared by introducing Al3+ ions to prevent aluminum loss. These nanosheets act as photodynamic/photothermal agents for cancer therapy. They exhibit an excellent mass extinction coefficient of 28.6 Lg−1cm−1, a significant photothermal conversion efficiency of 58.3%, and substantial singlet oxygen production of 102 under 808 nm laser radiation. Ti3C2 nanosheets have proven to be capable of generating reactive oxygen species, making them a promising option as photosensitizer agents for cancer treatment using photodynamic therapy, see Fig. 20 [225].

Ti3C2Tx MXenes loaded with indocyanine green (ICG) have been developed to merge the photothermal effect of MXene with the photodynamic effect of ICG. In the absence of near-infrared (NIR) irradiation, neither MXene, ICG, nor ICG-loaded MXene exhibited detectable antibacterial activity against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). However, under NIR irradiation, the viability loss of MRSA significantly increased, reaching 45% for MXene, 66% for ICG, and 100% for ICG-MXene, see Fig. 21 [226].

A photothermal/photodynamic synergistic antibacterial nanocomposite material called Ru@MXene was constructed by linking Ru(bpy)(dcb)2+ to MXene nanosheets through ester bonding. These nanoparticles display stronger antibacterial properties than Ru or MXene alone due to the enhanced production of reactive oxygen species and the heating effect. Ru@MXene demonstrated rapid and effective bactericidal activity against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus upon exposure to a xenon lamp, leveraging synergistic photothermal therapy and photodynamic therapy abilities [227].

To enhance the effectiveness of chemotherapy, phototherapy, and enzyme dynamic therapy against tumors, an intelligent bionic cascaded-enzyme nanoreactor, called meTGCT, was developed. Research has shown that the meTGCT nanoreactor remains stable over long periods both in vitro and in vivo. Under laser irradiation, the cascade enzyme nanoreactor utilizes glucose and molecular oxygen at tumor sites to provide tumor phototherapy/enzyme dynamic therapy. Furthermore, it creates a more hypoxic tumor microenvironment, which facilitates chemotherapy [228].

MXenes, 2D nanoparticles, have found extensive applications in various cancer therapies and treatments. Some examples including phototherapy, drug delivery, biosensing, and nano-enzyme-based treatments are described.

Fluorine-free methods have been employed to prepare Ti3C2 quantum dots (QDs), which serve as biocompatible and effective nanoagents for photothermal therapy (PTT). These synthetic MXene QDs exhibit a photothermal conversion efficiency of approximately 52.2% and an extinction coefficient of 52.81 g−1cm−1 at 808 nm [229], see Fig. 22 .

MXenes possess numerous functional groups and a significant surface area, making them suitable for drug loading and effective delivery to tumor sites. MXenes-FA-SP@DOX is a drug carrier system that demonstrates 69.9% drug loading capacity and sustained release, enhancing cancer treatment compared to the drug alone [230].
Gold/Ti3C2Tx-coated photonic crystal fiber (PCF) forms a surface plasmon resonance sensor, which plays a significant role in cancer cell recognition [231].

Ti3C2MXene nanosheets modified with near-infrared responsive IR 780 are utilized for the treatment of laryngeal cancer. This approach combines the benefits of photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), leading to mitochondrial pathway apoptosis and cancer cell death. Ti3C2@IR780 generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and achieves a cancer cell death rate of up to 60.51%. It also effectively accumulates at the tumor site, increases the temperature to 63.7% in the presence of infrared (IR) radiation, and reduces tumor growth by 92% [232].
Surface-functionalized tantalum carbide T4C3MXene, termed MXene (T4C3-IOP-SPs), plays a crucial role in effective breast cancer therapeutics [233], see Fig. 23 .

An electrochemical aptasensor based on a cDNA-ferrocene/MXene probe is utilized to detect the breast cancer marker Mucin 1 (MUC1). The MXene (Ti3C2) nanosheets, known for their excellent electrical conductivity and surface area, serve as carriers for the aptamer probe assembly [234].

MXene-derived quantum dots with a glutathione cap (GSH-MQDs) are employed in a magnetic biomimetic vesicle-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for the detection of miRNA221 in tumor tissue from breast cancer patients. The GSH-MQDs exhibit enhanced stability and luminous qualities as ECL emitters due to the use of glutathione as a precursor and the synergistic effects between the sulfhydryl group of GSH and the metal atoms on MXene [235].

A defect-based 1D photonic crystal (phC) model coated with Ti3C2TxMXene is developed for the detection of various cancer cell types, such as Jurkat, MCF-7, and basal cells. The optimization of various geometrical parameters, including the thickness of MXene, metamaterial, SiO2 layers, defect layer thickness, and the number of phC periods, ensures high performance [236].

A surface-engineered vanadium carbide MXene nano-enzyme, Ga/V2C-NH2, with amino functionalization and gallium doping, is designed to prevent the progression from colitis to colorectal cancer and provide effective anticancer therapy. MXenes exhibit potent reactive oxygen species (ROS) activity and control the level of proinflammatory cytokines, leading to anti-inflammatory effects in acute colitis. Gallium-doped MXene with amino functionalization enhances MXene-tumor interactions and inhibitory effects on cell proliferation, enabling accurate and effective chemo-photothermal therapy [237].

In response to a range of human bone injuries, such as fractures and bone cancer, there has been a growing focus on the development of innovative biomaterials for the repair and restoration of damaged or missing bone tissue. One of the significant challenges lies in the preparation of bio-scaffolds using bone-inducing compounds to effectively address bone defects. Notably, MAX-phases and MXenes have garnered considerable attention in this field due to their distinctive hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and photothermal properties. Researchers are actively exploring various approaches to combine MAX-phase MXenes, such as additive manufacturing (AM) and 3D printing with 2D materials, to create cost-effective and rapid scaffold manufacturing methods [238].
These studies demonstrate the significant potential of MXenes in nanotechnology-based cancer treatments, including therapy, diagnosis, and tumor eradication.

Exposure of metal atoms can lead to thermodynamic instability in MXenes. In such cases, these metal atoms can readily react with trace oxygen-containing groups, ultimately forming the corresponding metal oxides. This phenomenon results in a sudden decrease in the materials' activities and the loss of their superior physicochemical properties. To address this issue, stable and effective MXenes-based materials have been developed by introducing intercalation and chemical modifications of surface functional groups, often combined with specific synergistic additives. Importantly, these modifications can be achieved under non-hazardous conditions, yielding materials with remarkable optical, electrical, and magnetic properties [239].

MXenes stability can be improved by following ways:
Optimizing MAX Phase Synthesis: By increasing the number of compounds containing carbon/nitrogen and transition metals or by adjusting the C/N ratio in the MAX phase, the stability and performance of MXenes produced via etching can be enhanced. Integration with other materials to form heterojunctions is another effective method, involving combining the MAX phase with other materials before etching and exfoliation. An example is the use of MAX/C3N4 composites, where the numerous N atoms in C3N4 create a local negative charge on the MAX surface. This process results in MXenes/C3N4 heterojunctions with excellent stability and functionality.
Modification of MXenes Synthesis: Aqueous chemical exfoliation, while a primary method for creating MXenes, may introduce surface flaws, functional complexes, and incomplete reaction products. Using non-aqueous solvents for MXenes preparation and employing chemical vapor deposition for growing thin MXenes can be potential replacements. For example, MXenes-Ta2CS2, prepared through ordered termination with sulfur atoms in a single-step process, eliminates the need for etching and precursors with aluminum, greatly improving stability and electrochemical properties.
Post-Processing of MXenes: The electronic structure of MXenes can be altered by defects and the replacement of carbon with nitrogen that increase their stability and activity. As MXenes’ positively charged edges are where oxidation begins joining their edges with material that is negatively charged may be more stable. For instance, edges of negatively charged carbon quantum dots anchored on MXenes sheet may be able to stop those edges from oxidizing. MXenes oxidation is mainly due to presence of water and oxygen on the terminal hydrophilic groups is converted into hydrophobic. Similarly, stability can also be enhanced by intercalation; interaction among MXenes sheets and intercalator [240].

ce:para.ce:bold: MXenes and other 2D and 3D Materials in hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:section.ce:para:
MXenes, particularly those with porous surfaces, possess several advantageous properties that make them valuable for hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) and related applications:
Electrical Conductivity: MXenes exhibit excellent electrical conductivity, making them suitable for catalytic applications.
Ion/Guest Control: Their porous structure allows for efficient control over ions and guest molecules, enhancing catalytic performance.
Permittivity and Electromagnetic Transmission: MXenes' properties, such as permittivity and electromagnetic transmission, further contribute to their suitability for catalysis and other applications.
Support for Functional Materials: MXenes can serve as a platform to host and support various functional materials, enabling the development of advanced catalysts.

One example of this is the development of Ti3CN (OH)x@MoS2 hybrids through etching and post-hydrothermal methods, which serve as effective catalysts for maximizing H2 evolution [241]. Additionally, other materials, such as porous 3D carbon, have shown promise as novel materials for hydrogen production. 3D interlinked porous MXenes, characterized by ultrathin holes, enhance the electronic structure and demonstrate good hydrogen evolution performance. Examples include the Ti3C2TX-RGO network layered with LDHs and Co2P/N@Ti3C2Tx@NF [242].

Furthermore, 2D materials like sulfur-doped g-C3N4/defective g-C3N4 isotype S-scheme heterojunctions play a crucial role in photocatalytic H2 evolution [243]. The preparation of sulfur-doped g-C3N4/defective g-C3N4 isotype S-scheme heterojunctions in situ enhances the photocatalytic H2 evolution process [244]. Additionally, 2D g-C3N4/CuO nanocomposites have been discovered as ultra-sensitive electrochemical sensors for detecting dopamine, showcasing the versatility of 2D materials in various applications [245].

Indeed, the strategic development of MXenes and their composites for cancer nano-theranostics is a crucial challenge in the field of biomedicine. Incorporating various therapeutic and imaging capabilities into MXenes is essential for effective cancer treatment. Surface modification and hybridization techniques can play a significant role in enhancing the properties of MXenes to make them more suitable for theranostic applications, including radiotherapy, catalytic therapy, photothermal therapy, and imaging.

Future research should go beyond Ti3C2MXene and explore other MXene types while considering optimal conditions and functionalization methods. While MXenes have shown promise in photothermal cancer nano-therapy, improving their cellular internalization by coating their surfaces with ligands that specifically target cancer cells is an important area to explore. By doing so, researchers can enhance the efficacy of MXenes in targeted cancer treatment.

The next generation of nano-therapeutics is embracing a diverse range of 2D materials that extend beyond graphene [246]. Notably, 2D early transition-metal carbides/nitrides, often referred to as 2D MXenes [247,248], and their most recent counterparts, 2D transition-metal borides (known as 2D MBenes), have become pivotal in recent breakthroughs. These 2D materials hold immense promise, combining unique properties with the core functionalities of traditional drug delivery agents.
