This synergy positions them to potentially overcome the significant challenges associated with their biotechnological applications [249].

MBenes have introduced a new category of nanostructures within the broader MXene family by replacing nitrogen (N) or carbon (C) with boron (B). However, it's important to recognize that the MAB-MBene groups cannot be directly compared to the related MAX-MXene structures due to their distinct stoichiometries, variable configurations of 2D layers, and diverse forms of structural modifications. These distinctions become evident when considering their unique structural and chemical characteristics [250,251].

In contrast to MXenes, MBenes exhibit exceptional bioactivity, owing to their inclusion of benign boron. Boron is regarded as an essential trace element critical for the proper functioning of living organisms [252]. Boron plays a pivotal role in modulating metabolic processes and possesses anti-inflammatory properties [253]. The latter is attributed to its ability to inhibit phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4), a proinflammatory cyclic nucleotide associated with the release of cytokines that contribute to inflammation. This characteristic can be harnessed in drug delivery systems aimed at treating disorders associated with inflammation. MBenes find applications as biocidal agents in various contexts and may also display antibacterial or biostatic properties [252,254].

MXenes have already made significant contributions in various areas of cancer theranostics, including imaging technologies, biosensing, chemotherapy, photothermal therapy, and photodynamic therapy. The unique structure, large surface area, high electrical conductivity, extraordinary stability, and optical and mechanical properties of MXenes make them highly suitable for cancer treatment and other biomedical applications.

Further investigations are needed in areas such as high-rate electrochemical energy storage, soft robotics, wearable sensors, catalytic reactions, and EMI shielding films. The development of wearable and stretchable strain sensors with high sensitivity and adjustable sensing range using MXene composites should be prioritized. Detailed evaluations of MXene hybrids, considering factors such as specific surface area, density, pore volume, and electrical, mechanical, and thermal properties, are essential to fully unlock their potential in diverse fields of application.

In terms of synthesis, there is a need for environmentally friendly, fluorine-free, cost-effective, and simplified methods to produce MXenes with higher yields. Optimization and functionalization techniques should be advanced to enhance the stability, functionality, and biosafety of MXenes. Additionally, efforts should focus on synthesizing MXene structures that have been proposed theoretically but not yet successfully synthesized in the laboratory. Thorough studies on the cytotoxicity of MXenes are also necessary to ensure their versatile use in therapeutic and imaging techniques.

By addressing these challenges and exploring the potential of MXenes in biomedical applications, significant advancements can be made in oncology research and cancer treatment. The future of MXenes in biomedicine holds great promise, and continued research and innovation in this field will contribute to improved cancer theranostics and other biomedical applications.

MXenes, being a new class of nanomaterials, are still in the early stages of development for use in oncology, which involves the study and treatment of cancer. However, researchers from around the world have made significant progress by investigating various ways to modify and enhance MXenes for specific cancer types and treatment approaches, yielding encouraging results.

One area of focus is the use of MXenes in combination with imaging agents, which can have beneficial outcomes in cancer theranostics. Theranostics refers to the integration of diagnostics and therapeutics, where a single agent or system is capable of both diagnosing and treating a disease. By incorporating MXenes with imaging agents, researchers aim to develop powerful tools for both detecting and treating cancer. This can potentially lead to significant advancements in cancer investigation and personalized treatment strategies.

The search for prospective MXenes for medical applications, particularly in cancer treatment, is driven by their intriguing biological compatibility and the ability to tailor their electrical and optical properties. MXenes have demonstrated promising outcomes in both laboratory settings (in vitro) and animal models (in vivo) of cancer. These studies have shown the potential of MXenes to effectively target cancer cells, deliver therapeutic agents, and enhance the efficacy of treatment methods.

As research in this field progresses, scientists are exploring various strategies to further optimize MXenes for cancer theranostics. This involves investigating different functionalization's and compositions of MXenes to enhance their targeting capabilities, drug delivery efficiency, and imaging properties. By fine-tuning the properties of MXenes, researchers aim to develop more effective and precise diagnostic and therapeutic approaches for cancer.

It is important to note that while MXenes hold promise, they are still in the early stages of development, and further research is needed to fully understand their potential in oncology and other biomedical applications. Ongoing studies are focused on elucidating the cytotoxicity and long-term effects of MXenes, as well as developing environmentally friendly and cost-effective methods for their production.

Overall, the research and development of MXenes in oncology and cancer theranostics is a promising area of investigation. Continued efforts in exploring the properties, functionalization's, and compositions of MXenes will contribute to their further advancement and potential application in diagnosing and treating cancer, ultimately leading to improved outcomes for patients.
